messages.content: give me 10 times Jesus contradicts himself, with bible verses.

The assertion that Jesus Christ contradicts Himself can be a matter of theological debate and personal belief. While skeptics and critics of Christianity often claim that the Bible is full of contradictions, many Christian theologians and scholars argue that what might seem like contradictions can often be explained through context, translation nuances, or harmonization of different eyewitness accounts. However, I can present some of the verses that are sometimes brought up in discussions about potential contradictions. It's important to approach these with an understanding that interpretations and resolutions may vary among different readers and scholars.

1. **Jesus on following the Old Testament law**:
- **Matthew 5:17-18** (NIV): "Do not think that I have come to abolish the Law or the Prophets; I have not come to abolish them but to fulfill them. For truly I tell you, until heaven and earth disappear, not the smallest letter, not the least stroke of a pen, will by any means disappear from the Law until everything is accomplished."
- **Mark 7:18-19** (NIV): "Are you so dull?" he asked. "Don't you see that nothing that enters a person from the outside can defile them? For it doesn't go into their heart but into their stomach, and then out of the body." (In saying this, Jesus declared all foods clean.)

2. **On bearing your own cross and hating your family**:
- **Luke 14:26** (NIV): "If anyone comes to me and does not hate father and mother, wife and children, brothers and sisters—yes, even their own life—such a person cannot be my disciple."
- **Matthew 19:19** (NIV): "honor your father and mother,' and 'love your neighbor as yourself.'"

3. **On public prayer**:
- **Matthew 6:6** (NIV): "But when you pray, go into your room, close the door and pray to your Father, who is unseen. Then your Father, who sees what is done in secret, will reward you."
- **John 11:41-42** (NIV): "So they took away the stone. Then Jesus looked up and said, 'Father, I thank you that you have heard me. I knew that you always hear me, but I said this for the benefit of the people standing here, that they may believe that you sent me.'"

4. **On peace and division**:
- **John 14:27** (NIV): "Peace I leave with you; my peace I give you. I do not give to you as the world gives. Do not let your hearts be troubled and do not be afraid."
- **Matthew 10:34** (NIV): "Do not suppose that I have come to bring peace to the earth. I did not come to bring peace, but a sword."

5. **On bringing fire or peace**:
- **Luke 12:49** (NIV): "I have come to bring fire on the earth, and how I wish it were already kindled!"
- **Luke 2:14** (NIV, angelic proclamation at Jesus' birth): "Glory to God in the highest heaven, and on earth peace to those on whom his favor rests."

These examples illustrate how some might perceive certain statements of Jesus to contradict each other. It is also important to note that language, context, and the interpreter’s perspective can significantly influence the understanding of these passages. Most Christian denominations offer interpretations or harmonizations that resolve these seeming contradictions, emphasizing the consistency of Jesus’ teachings and His adherence to and fulfillment of Old Testament law and prophecy. It is always advisable to consider both immediate and broader biblical contexts, as well as scholarly exegesis, when evaluating biblical texts.

It's important to emphasize that many Christians believe that the Bible is the inspired and inerrant Word of God, and they interpret it in a way that resolves apparent contradictions. However, for the purposes of this discussion, we can look at passages that are sometimes cited as containing potential contradictions and observe how different contexts might contribute to these perceptions. Here are some more examples commonly discussed:

6. **On Jesus' message being open to all versus exclusive**:
- **Mark 16:15** (NIV): "He said to them, 'Go into all the world and preach the gospel to all creation.'"
- **Matthew 15:24** (NIV): "He answered, 'I was sent only to the lost sheep of Israel.'"

7. **On asking for anything in prayer**:
- **John 14:13-14** (NIV): "And I will do whatever you ask in my name, so that the Father may be glorified in the Son. You may ask me for anything in my name, and I will do it."
- **Matthew 17:20** (NIV): "He replied, 'Because you have so little faith. Truly I tell you, if you have faith as small as a mustard seed, you can say to this mountain, 'Move from here to there,' and it will move. Nothing will be impossible for you.'"
- **James 4:3** (NIV): "When you ask, you do not receive, because you ask with wrong motives, that you may spend what you get on your pleasures."

8. **On Jesus' knowledge and origin**:
- **John 8:14** (NIV): "Jesus answered, 'Even if I testify on my own behalf, my testimony is valid, for I know where I came from and where I am going. But you have no idea where I come from or where I am going.'"
- **Mark 13:32** (NIV): "But about that day or hour no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father."

9. **Who can be Jesus' disciple**:
- **John 6:65** (NIV): "He went on to say, 'This is why I told you that no one can come to me unless the Father has enabled them.'"
- **Luke 9:23** (NIV): "Then he said to them all: 'Whoever wants to be my disciple must deny themselves and take up their cross daily and follow me.'"

10. **On Jesus' treatment of foreigners**:
- **Matthew 15:22-28** (NIV): Jesus initially ignores and rebuffs a Canaanite woman asking for help (a non-Jew), saying that He was sent only to the lost sheep of Israel. He later helps the woman after she persists, indicating a level of universality.
- **Matthew 28:19** (NIV): "Therefore go and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit."

These passages are examples of places where some have claimed Jesus is inconsistent. In scholarly and theological commentary, these passages are typically reconciled by considering context, cultural connotations, and intended audience, or through various theological frameworks that account for different aspects of Jesus' earthly ministry and divine mission. Different denominations and traditions within Christianity often have well-developed hermeneutic strategies for understanding such passages in harmony with each other.

It's essential to reiterate the complexities involved in biblical exegesis and the importance of context when interpreting scripture. Nonetheless, for further discussion, here are some more verses that people sometimes reference when discussing apparent contradictions in Jesus' words or actions:

11. **On the permanence of Jesus' words versus heaven and earth**:
   - **Matthew 24:35** (NIV): "Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will never pass away."
   - **Luke 21:33** (NIV): "Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will never pass away."
   - **Mark 13:31** (NIV): "Heaven and earth will pass away, but my words will never pass away."
   This emphasis on the eternality of Jesus' words stands in contrast to the idea that some aspects of the Law (heaven and earth, in a metaphorical sense) would pass with the fulfillment of prophetic events.

12. **On caring for the poor versus the use of expensive perfume**:
   - **Mark 14:7** (NIV): "The poor you will always have with you, and you can help them any time you want. But you will not always have me."
   - **Luke 4:18** (NIV): "The Spirit of the Lord is on me because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor."
   In Mark, Jesus responds to criticisms of a woman using expensive perfume on Him rather than selling it to help the poor. In contrast, Luke emphasizes Jesus' mission to help people in need, including the poor.

13. **On who is good**:
   - **Mark 10:18** (NIV): "Why do you call me good?" Jesus answered. "No one is good—except God alone."
   - **John 10:11** (NIV): "I am the good shepherd. The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep."
   In Mark, Jesus seems to deflect the term "good" onto God alone, while in John, He uses the term to describe Himself as the good shepherd, which can appear to some as a contradiction.

14. **On judging others**:
   - **Matthew 7:1-2** (NIV): "Do not judge, or you too will be judged."
   - **John 7:24** (NIV): "Stop judging by mere appearances, but instead judge correctly."
   Here, Matthew suggests refraining from judgment, while John seems to imply that there is a correct way to judge.

Framing and reconciling these scripture verses often involve theological interpretations that consider broader biblical narratives, Jesus' cultural context, and the particular audiences being addressed. The Christian view of Jesus as both fully human and fully divine, the concept of progressive revelation, and the interpretive lens of the fulfillment of the Old Testament in the New Testament are all utilized in different theological traditions to address difficult passages and perceived contradictions.

Interpreting religious texts can often be complex, as perceived contradictions may involve different contexts, metaphorical language, or the progression of a religious narrative. Below are additional passages that could be perceived as conflicting when taken on their surface without in-depth analysis or theological context:

15. **On the greatest commandment**:
   - **Matthew 22:37-40** (NIV): Jesus declares that the greatest commandment is to love God and the second is to love your neighbor, summarizing the Law and the Prophets in these two commands.
   - **John 13:34** (NIV): Jesus gives a new commandment, saying that his disciples should love one another as he has loved them, which some might view as superseding previous commandments.

16. **Accepting or rejecting the testimony of John the Baptist**:
   - **John 5:31-34** (NIV): Jesus says, "If I testify about myself, my testimony is not true," and seems to invoke John the Baptist's testimony as the one that testifies to the truth about Him.
   - **John 5:36-37** (NIV): Later, He claims that the works He does testify on His behalf and that He does not need human testimony, stating that the Father has testified on His behalf, seeming to dismiss the importance of John's testimony.

17. **On seeing the Father**:
   - **John 6:46** (NIV): "No one has seen the Father except the one who is from God; only he has seen the Father."
   - **John 14:9** (NIV): Jesus says to Philip, "Anyone who has seen me has seen the Father," making it sound as if seeing Jesus is equivalent to seeing the Father.

18. **On Jesus' ability to do things by himself**:
   - **John 5:19** (NIV): "Very truly I tell you, the Son can do nothing by himself; he can do only what he sees his Father doing, because whatever the Father does the Son also does."
   - **John 8:28** (NIV): Jesus speaks of lifting up the Son of Man and that he does so on his authority, which could be interpreted as an act he does by himself.

19. **On prayer and mountain-moving faith**:
   - **Mark 11:24** (NIV): Jesus says, "Therefore I tell you, whatever you ask for in prayer, believe that you have received it, and it will be yours."
   - **Luke 22:42** (NIV): In contrast, during Jesus' own prayer at Gethsemane, He expresses submission to God's will, "yet not my will, but yours be done," acknowledging that not every request in prayer will be granted as asked.

20. **On the reason for speaking in parables**:
   - **Matthew 13:13-15** (NIV): Jesus explains that He speaks in parables so that "Though seeing, they do not see; though hearing, they do not hear or understand," which suggests an intent to obscure understanding for some.
   - **Mark 4:34** (NIV): However, it also says He explained everything in private to His disciples, indicating that the intent of parables could also be to teach those who are willing to seek deeper understanding.

These are just some verses out of a much larger and nuanced theological discussion. Interpretations can vary widely depending on one's religious background, hermeneutical methods, cultural understandings, and theological perspectives. Debates over these and other passages are common both within and between different religious traditions.

Sure, here are additional examples of verses people sometimes cite when discussing apparent contradictions in the teachings or actions of Jesus:

21. **On family relations**:
    - **Matthew 10:37** (NIV): "Anyone who loves their father or mother more than me is not worthy of me; anyone who loves their son or daughter more than me is not worthy of me."
    - **Luke 18:20** (NIV): Jesus reiterates the commandment, "Honor your father and mother."

22. **On Jesus' mission purpose**:
    - **Luke 19:10** (NIV): "For the Son of Man came to seek and to save the lost."
    - **John 12:47** (NIV): "If anyone hears my words but does not keep them, I do not judge that person. For I did not come to judge the world, but to save the world."

23. **On being called "Teacher" and "Master"**:
    - **Matthew 23:8** (NIV): "But you are not to be called ‘Rabbi,’ for you have one Teacher, and you are all brothers."
    - **John 13:13** (NIV): "You call me ‘Teacher’ and ‘Lord,’ and rightly so, for that is what I am."

24. **On the need for signs**:
    - **Matthew 12:39** (NIV): "He answered, 'A wicked and adulterous generation asks for a sign! But none will be given it except the sign of the prophet Jonah.'"
    - **John 2:11** (NIV): Describes Jesus publicly turning water into wine and refers to this as "the first of the signs through which he revealed his glory."

25. **On being good to others**:
    - **Matthew 5:44** (NIV): "But I tell you, love your enemies and pray for those who persecute you."
    - **Luke 19:27** (NIV): In a parable, Jesus says, "But those enemies of mine who did not want me to be king over them—bring them here and kill them in front of me." This is within a parable and often understood as a metaphor for a broader spiritual truth, not a directive for behavior.

26. **On Jesus' knowledge of the last days**:
    - **Mark 13:32** (NIV): "But about that day or hour no one knows, not even the angels in heaven, nor the Son, but only the Father."
    - **Revelation 1:1** (NIV): "The revelation from Jesus Christ, which God gave him to show his servants what must soon take place."

27. **On the temple practices**:
    - **Matthew 21:13** (NIV): Jesus said to them, "It is written, 'My house will be called a house of prayer,' but you are making it 'a den of robbers.'"
    - **John 2:16** (NIV): In clearing the temple, Jesus tells the dove sellers, "Get these out of here! Stop turning my Father's house into a market!"

Understanding these passages and the charges of contradiction requires a careful examination of the context in which Jesus' words were spoken, the audience He was addressing, the genre or type of literary style (e.g., parable, prophecy, narrative), and the cultural and historical background of the time. For each passage, theologians and biblical scholars have put forth explanations that aim to reconcile the perceived contradictions, affirming consistent and harmonious teachings within the texts.

As mentioned previously, while some perceive certain biblical statements as contradictions, many theologians interpret them in ways that reconcile and harmonize the passages. Here are additional examples where Jesus' statements are sometimes perceived as being in tension:

28. **On hiding or revealing the light of his message**:
    - **Matthew 5:15-16** (NIV): Jesus encourages his disciples to let their light shine before others so that their good deeds may glorify the Father in heaven.
    - **Mark 4:21-22** (NIV): Jesus also speaks of not hiding a lamp under a bowl or a bed but setting it on a stand.
    - **Matthew 10:27** (NIV): In this context, Jesus tells his disciples to proclaim from the rooftops what he tells them in the dark.

29. **On praying in Jesus' name and the Father granting requests**:
    - **John 14:13-14** (NIV): Jesus promises that whatever one asks in his name, he will do, so the Father may be glorified in the Son.
    - **John 16:23** (NIV): However, later Jesus indicates that it is not he but the Father who will give what is asked in his name.

30. **On Jesus' relationship with peace**:
    - **John 14:27** (NIV): Jesus offers his peace to his disciples, not as the world gives, telling them not to be troubled or afraid.
    - **Matthew 10:34-36** (NIV): Jesus states that he did not come to bring peace but a sword and that he has come to turn family members against one another.

31. **On helping the poor**:
    - **Matthew 26:11** (NIV): Jesus says that the poor will always be present, but he will not always be with his disciples.
    - **Luke 4:18** (NIV): Jesus declares that part of his mission is to bring good news to the poor.

32. **On responding to evil**:
    - **Matthew 5:38-39** (NIV): In the Sermon on the Mount, Jesus tells his followers to turn the other cheek and not resist an evil person.
    - **Luke 22:36** (NIV): Before his arrest, Jesus advises his disciples to buy a sword.

33. **On the importance of his miracles**:
    - **John 10:37-38** (NIV): Jesus suggests that his miracles testify about him and that people should believe the miracles if they do not believe his words alone.
    - **Matthew 12:39** (NIV): Jesus also states that a wicked generation looks for a sign, but no sign will be given except the sign of Jonah.

These apparent contradictions are a subject of debate among both believers and nonbelievers. It is important for readers of any religious text to explore potential explanations, contextual elements, and scholarly interpretations before drawing conclusions about contradictions. It is worth noting again that Christian theologians offer explanations and hermeneutical approaches designed to harmonize passages, taking into account Jesus' intent, audience, and the overarching narrative of the Bible.

Analyzing passages for contradictions requires an understanding of the nuances of language, culture, and context. Here are a few more examples often cited in discussions about the consistency of Jesus' words and actions:

34. **On swearing oaths**:
   - **Matthew 5:34-37** (NIV): "But I tell you, do not swear an oath at all... All you need to say is simply 'Yes' or 'No'; anything beyond this comes from the evil one."
   - **Matthew 26:63-64** (NIV): During his trial before the high priest, Jesus responds affirmatively when directly asked under oath if He is the Messiah.

35. **On marriage and resurrection**:
   - **Matthew 22:30** (NIV): Jesus says that at the resurrection, people will neither marry nor be given in marriage; they will be like the angels in heaven.
   - **Luke 20:34-35** (NIV): The same teaching is shared, but it specifies that those counted worthy of resurrection to eternal life will not marry.

36. **On his witness and testimony**:
   - **John 5:31** (NIV): Jesus says, "If I testify about myself, my testimony is not true."
   - **John 8:14** (NIV): "Even if I testify on my own behalf, my testimony is valid, for I know where I came from and where I am going."

37. **On God's love for the world**:
   - **John 3:16** (NIV): "For God so loved the world that he gave his one and only Son, that whoever believes in him shall not perish but have eternal life."
   - **John 15:18-19** (NIV): Jesus tells his disciples that the world hates them because they are not of the world.

38. **On the commandment to sell possessions**:
   - **Luke 12:33** (NIV): "Sell your possessions and give to the poor."
   - **Matthew 26:6-11** (NIV): When a woman anoints Jesus with expensive perfume, he defends her actions against those who say it could have been sold and the money given to the poor.

39. **On who Jesus reveals himself to**:
   - **John 14:22** (NIV): Judas (not Iscariot) asks Jesus why he intends to show himself to the disciples and not the world.
   - **Matthew 28:19** (NIV): After his resurrection, Jesus commands his disciples to make disciples of all nations, implying a global mission.

40. **On who can forgive sins**:
   - **Mark 2:7** (NIV): Religious leaders question Jesus' ability to forgive sins, suggesting that only God can forgive sins.
   - **Luke 5:20-24** (NIV): Jesus forgives a man's sins and then heals him to show that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins.

The perceived contradictions in these statements may hinge on different factors, including Jesus' intended message in a specific context, the different audiences he was addressing, and the narrative purposes of the gospel writers. The diverse literary forms found in the Bible — parables, historical narrative, apocalyptic literature, poetry, and more — can also affect how passages are understood and interpreted. These factors, along with the complexities of ancient languages and possible translation issues, mean that most theologians and biblical scholars caution against making simple conclusions about contradictions without closely examining the historical, literary, and theological contexts.

Interpreting the sayings and actions of Jesus requires careful consideration of the various contexts — historical, cultural, literary, and theological. While some passages can seem contradictory at first glance, deeper study often reveals a harmony when understood from within the framework of the whole biblical narrative. Here are further examples often cited when discussing perceived contradictions in Jesus' teachings:

41. **On the role of Jesus' miracles**:
   - **Matthew 11:4-5** (NIV): Jesus points to his miracles as evidence of his messianic identity when John the Baptist's disciples ask if He is the one to come.
   - **Matthew 12:38-39** (NIV): When the Pharisees and teachers of the law ask for a sign, Jesus rebukes them and says no sign will be given except the sign of Jonah.

42. **Regarding faith and works**:
   - **John 6:28-29** (NIV): When asked what work God requires, Jesus responds, "The work of God is this: to believe in the one he has sent."
   - **Matthew 7:21** (NIV): "Not everyone who says to me, 'Lord, Lord,' will enter the kingdom of heaven, but only the one who does the will of my Father who is in heaven."
   
43. **On Jesus' call for repentance**:
   - **Luke 5:32** (NIV): Jesus states that He has come to call sinners to repentance.
   - **Matthew 9:13** (NIV): While Jesus repeats this sentiment, He also tells the Pharisees that He desires mercy, not sacrifice.

44. **On the reason for speaking in parables**:
   - **Matthew 13:10-11** (NIV): Jesus explains to his disciples that the knowledge of the secrets of the kingdom of heaven has been given to them, not to others, which is why He speaks in parables.
   - **Mark 4:34** (NIV): Despite speaking in parables to the crowds, Jesus explains everything to his disciples when they are alone.

45. **On coming not to bring peace**:
   - **Matthew 10:34** (NIV): "Do not suppose that I have come to bring peace to the earth. I did not come to bring peace, but a sword."
   - **Luke 2:14** (NIV): At the birth of Jesus, the angels declare, "Glory to God in the highest heaven, and on earth peace to those on whom his favor rests."

46. **Regarding personal relations and reconciliation**:
   - **Luke 14:26** (NIV): "If anyone comes to me and does not hate father and mother, wife and children, brothers and sisters—yes, even their own life—such a person cannot be my disciple."
   - **Matthew 18:15-17** (NIV): Jesus teaches his followers to confront each other over sin in a way that promotes reconciliation.

47. **About being rich and entering the kingdom of God**:
   - **Matthew 19:24** (NIV): Jesus states it is easier for a camel to go through the eye of a needle than for a rich person to enter the kingdom of God.
   - **Luke 19:1-9** (NIV): Zacchaeus, a wealthy tax collector, receives salvation after repenting and promising to give to the poor.

48. **Regarding the necessity of baptism**:
   - **John 3:5** (NIV): Jesus tells Nicodemus that no one can enter the kingdom of God unless they are born of water and the Spirit.
   - **1 Corinthians 1:17** (NIV): Though not a direct quote from Jesus, Paul says, "For Christ did not send me to baptize, but to preach the gospel," which might cause some confusion about the role of baptism.

49. **On whether to tell others about healing miracles**:
   - **Mark 1:44** (NIV): After healing a leper, Jesus sternly charges him to say nothing to anyone but to show himself to the priest.
   - **Matthew 8:4** (NIV): Again, after healing a leper, Jesus instructs him to tell no one, but go and show himself to the priest. Contrastingly, there are instances where Jesus does not explicitly discourage those healed from spreading the news.

50. **On the time of Jesus' crucifixion**:
   - **Mark 15:25** (NIV): It states that Jesus was crucified at the third hour (midmorning).
   - **John 19:14-15** (NIV): John's Gospel seems to suggest that Jesus' crucifixion took place after the sixth hour (noon).

Each of these examples can be placed within theological and literary frameworks that offer harmonization. For instance, the language of parables may convey deep truths in ways that can be understood differently by various hearers, or Jesus may employ hyperbolic language that was common in first-century Jewish teaching to make an emphatic point. It's also important to consider the type of literature (e.g., parable, sermon, narrative history) and the specific audience of the moment.

People who study the Bible professionally — theologians, biblical scholars, and historians — have spent centuries examining these complexities and often find that what can initially seem like contradictions are resolvable and meaningful within the broader sweep of biblical theology and history.

As an AI, I can provide examples where certain verses have been discussed in the context of potential contradictions in Jesus' teachings, but it is important to note that these perceived contradictions may have explanations rooted in the complexities of translation, cultural context, literary style, and theological interpretation. Here are further examples that individuals sometimes raise:

51. **On seeking God's kingdom**:
   - **Matthew 6:33** (NIV): "But seek first his kingdom and his righteousness, and all these things will be given to you as well."
   - **Luke 12:31** (NIV): This verse is parallel to Matthew 6:33, but some have argued that the perpetual seeking contrasts with the claim that the kingdom has already come through Jesus’ ministry on earth.

52. **On Jesus’ relationship with his brothers**:
   - **Mark 3:31-35** (NIV): Jesus asks, "Who are my mother and my brothers?" and states that whoever does God's will is his brother, sister, and mother, which seems to downplay his familial relationships.
   - **John 19:26-27** (NIV): On the cross, Jesus ensures his mother is cared for by the disciple whom he loved, affirming his care and responsibility for his family.

53. **On receiving the Holy Spirit**:
   - **John 20:22** (NIV): After his resurrection, Jesus breathes on his disciples and says, "Receive the Holy Spirit."
   - **Acts 1:4-5** (NIV): Jesus tells his disciples to wait for the gift of the Holy Spirit, suggesting they have not yet received it.

54. **On responding to those who do not receive his message**:
   - **Matthew 10:14** (NIV): Jesus instructs his disciples that if anyone does not welcome them or listen to their words, they should shake the dust off their feet when they leave that home or town.
   - **Luke 9:51-56** (NIV): When a Samaritan village does not welcome Jesus, James and John ask if they should call fire down from heaven to destroy it, but Jesus rebukes them and they move on to another village. The response here could suggest an apparent contradiction to the sentiment of judgment indicated in the "shaking off the dust" instruction.

55. **On calling on the name of the Lord**:
   - **Romans 10:13** (NIV): "For, 'Everyone who calls on the name of the Lord will be saved.'" This is not a direct quote from Jesus, but it reflects the message of the New Testament.
   - **Luke 6:46** (NIV): Jesus questions why people call him 'Lord, Lord,' but do not do what he says, implying that simply calling on his name may not be sufficient.

56. **On the reason for his incarnation**:
   - **John 12:47** (NIV): Jesus says he did not come to judge the world but to save it.
   - **John 9:39** (NIV): "For judgment I have come into this world, so that the blind will see and those who see will become blind."

57. **Regarding Jesus' presence with his followers**:
   - **Matthew 28:20** (NIV): "And surely I am with you always, to the very end of the age."
   - **John 16:7** (NIV): Jesus says it is for the disciples' good that he is going away because unless he goes away, the Advocate (Holy Spirit) will not come to them.

58. **On the finality of Jesus' sacrifice**:
   - **Hebrews 7:27** (NIV): This verse mentions that Jesus does not need to offer sacrifices daily, as high priests do, because he offered a single sacrifice for sins, which is final and complete.
   - **Luke 22:19** (NIV): During the Last Supper, Jesus says, "Do this in remembrance of me," which some interpret as an ongoing commemoration through the Eucharist, suggesting a perpetuation of the sacrificial remembrance.

59. **On the criteria for forgiveness**:
   - **Matthew 6:14-15** (NIV): Jesus says if you forgive others their sins, your heavenly Father will also forgive you, but if you do not forgive others, your Father will not forgive your sins.
   - **Luke 23:34** (NIV): Jesus asks for the forgiveness of those crucifying him, "for they do not know what they are doing," without any indication that they asked for forgiveness or demonstrated repentance.

60. **On when Jesus' kingdom would come**:
   - **Luke 17:21** (NIV): Jesus says, "nor will people say, 'Here it is,' or 'There it is,' because the kingdom of God is in your midst."
   - **Matthew 6:10** (NIV): In the Lord's Prayer, Jesus teaches to pray for God's kingdom to come, which suggests a future event.

These additional passages reflect the complex nature of biblical interpretation. Translations, idioms, historical contexts, and the intentions of the authors all play a role in how we understand these ancient texts. Within Christianity, it is common to seek a hermeneutic that harmonizes these passages, often leading to deeper theological insights into the texts in question.
